video_title: The Thursday Murder Club | Official Trailer | Netflix
comment_text:
Though it was a mystery piece, it lacked any truly subversive twists that completely overturned expectations. In other words, it contained many rather clichéd elements. However, the film's true subject matter and core isn't the mystery itself. It's about characters entering their twilight years harnessing their special skills to strive for and pool their efforts towards what they love and what they need. 'Seniors can do anything!' It struck me as a blessing that, even as grandmothers and grandfathers, they know what they love and what they're good at, and above all, have companions and friends who support each other, enabling them to turn these things into action.

The difficulty level of the mystery wasn't high. Of course, the basic relationships between the characters weren't exactly simple, so I had to make an effort to grasp the names and characteristics of the characters who appeared in rapid succession early on. However, the subsequent unfolding of events (though I must admit I wasn't actively deducing as I watched, so I didn't predict any twists) felt like a repetition of a somewhat familiar formula, and the twists themselves were rather predictable. To be more specific, the degree of surprise was weak. In truth, my suspicions were aroused when the character Bogdan appeared  and Elizabeth approached him, and the outcome largely matched my hunch. Had I hoped for a truly shocking twist, he should not have been the culprit. Furthermore, the characters felt flat and overly virtuous, leaving a bland impression.

This marks director Chris Columbus's return to ensemble comedy and mystery after a long hiatus. From the outset, he aimed for a 'detective story with the humour and dignified warmth of old age', exploring later life and friendship through a 'cozy mystery' sensibility. Original author Richard Osmond actively participated in script meetings, advising to maintain 'a story where the elderly actively pursue justice rather than passively observing the world'. The director incorporated this advice, focusing more on character-driven narrative than the murder mystery itself.

Helen Mirren, playing Elizabeth, was dubbed 'the leader of the Thursday Club' on set. She led daily script readings with the cast before filming, and the director credited her with bringing the film's tone to life in interviews. Pierce Brosnan, as Ron, frequently improvised lines during filming, lifting the atmosphere on set. Some of these impromptu exchanges made it into the final cut unedited. Columbus revealed that his humor transformed the tense mystery into one filled with warm laughter. Ben Kingsley, renowned for his perfectionism that demands multiple takes for even the smallest gesture, practised deliberately botching tasks to capture the frail, human side of his elderly character. After the final shoot, Helen Mirren reflected on social media that the set felt more fun and liberating than any she'd experienced in her youth. Chris Columbus mentioned he hoped the film would remain, until the very end, not a puzzle but a letter of gratitude for life.

The main setting, Silver Town Cooper's Chase, was newly built on a set in the outskirts of Buckinghamshire, England, and operated like a community with around 200 real senior extras living there. Columbus explained he made the characters appear like a genuine community living together even off-camera. Compared to the original work, the level of violence was reduced, with greater emphasis placed on friendship, humor, the loneliness of old age, and recovery rather than the murder itself. He stated that the result was an intentional genre experiment aiming for a warm tension rather than pure horror.

video_title: The Thursday Murder Club | Official Trailer | Netflix
comment_text:
This.  Film. Was. Amazing. 
It starts out innocent, very jolly, gentle made-for-TV vibes, and then it gets very serious very fast. I did not expect such complex characters and drama. It’s actually quite deep—and *loads* of fun! 
It goes without saying, and yet I’ll say it—what a cast, what a director, what stellar performances! I have to say, especially the actor who played Bogdon. That’s a career-making performance.
Edit: Ron’s comment “that’s how I met your mother” was just SO off-putting. How am I supposed to not hate his character? It didn’t add anything to the plot. And instead of making it an *ffair, they could’ve just been together before the divorce was finalized, and that’s why it’s hush hush rather than a full-blown chav move

comment_text:
Damn, I just got my Netflix back, and was getting all excited, because I thought I would go watch it right now, and then it says it's August 28th, I wanted to watch it NOW, lol! 

This thing is loaded with so many great British stars, and a lot of my favorites too, I'll watch anything with Helen Mirren, she is amazing in everything! And we even have on of the Doctors, the 10th David Tennant! I also love British made movies or tv shows, thankfully Netflix has a ton of them, along with a lot of European shows and movies, all dubbed perfectly in English ( for those who only speak English, lol ) so we can all enjoy them, and I do. I actually tend to prefer non American made shows and movies, they aren't so over done, over dramatized, to the point that they come across so fake, they're is just not a lot of realism in American made shows and movies. Not all of them, but definitely a great deal of them.
